pmos

Product Discovery & User Research — Primer

Created using pmos‑learnkit

Primer · PM Tactics — Strategy. Product discovery is the discipline of deciding what to build before building it, and the modern canon is unusually unified on the why even where it splits on the how. The spine is Marty Cagan's four big risks — value, usability, feasibility, viability — and the rule that discovery exists to reduce them before engineering commits. The operating model is Teresa Torres's continuous discovery and her opportunity solution tree, which replaced the older dual-track agile framing of discovery and delivery as intertwined. The research substrate splits into generative versus evaluative work, and the lineage runs back through Steve Blank's customer development, Eric Ries's build-measure-learn, Gothelf and Seiden's Lean UX hypotheses, and David Bland's assumption mapping — with the riskiest-assumption test as the sharpest tool. Melissa Perri's build trap names the organizational failure all of this exists to prevent, and the confirmation-bias pitfalls are where good intentions go to die. Where practitioners genuinely disagree — how much discovery is enough, whether AI-cheap prototypes help or harm — this primer flags it. Provenance: practitioner-canon synthesis. 16 verified sources.

What discovery actually is

The cleanest definition of discovery is negative: it is everything you do to avoid building the wrong thing. Productboard's framing, drawing on Cagan, splits the work into two questions — "you need to discover whether there are real users out there that want this product" and then "a product solution to this problem that is usable, useful, and feasible." The first is about the problem; the second is about the solution. Both have to be answered before engineering capacity is committed, because the cost of answering them after launch is the entire build.

The load-bearing distinction in the modern canon is discovery versus delivery. Delivery is the work of building and shipping reliably; discovery is the work of deciding what is worth building at all. The two are different disciplines with different success criteria — delivery optimizes for predictability and quality, discovery optimizes for learning rate and risk reduction — and conflating them is the root error the rest of this primer keeps circling back to. As Productboard puts it plainly, "We conduct product discovery because we want to reduce the risks around what we decide to build."

What makes this a genuine discipline rather than a slogan is that discovery is not the same as having ideas. Ideas are cheap and abundant; the scarce thing is evidence that a particular idea solves a real problem in a way users will adopt and the business can sustain. Discovery is the systematic production of that evidence, and its defining move is sequencing: research and validate before committing to delivery, not the reverse. Teams that "research and validate concepts before sending them to engineering" are doing discovery; teams that ship first and learn from production are paying tuition.

The four big risks

The most useful single framework in discovery is Cagan's four big risks, because it converts the vague goal of "de-risk the idea" into four specific questions a team can actually attack. Value risk asks "Will customers buy it or users choose to use it." Usability risk asks "Can users figure out how to use it." Feasibility risk asks "Can engineering build it with the available time, skills, and tech." And business viability risk asks "Does it work for legal, finance, sales, marketing, and brand." Discovery, in Cagan's framing, has exactly one job: "Discovery exists to kill all four before delivery starts."

The four are not equal, and recognizing which one dominates is most of the skill. Value risk is usually the largest and the most neglected — teams that are confident they can build something are far less often confident anyone wants it, yet they spend their discovery effort on feasibility because it is the most tractable to a builder's instincts. The canon's advice is to spend the majority of discovery time wherever the risk profile is highest, which for most product teams is value, not the comfortable engineering questions.

The practical contribution of the framework is that it tells you what kind of evidence to go get. A value-risk question is answered by customer demand signals — interviews, fake-door tests, willingness-to-pay probes — not by a working prototype. A usability-risk question is answered by watching real users attempt real tasks. A feasibility-risk question is answered by an engineering spike, and a viability-risk question by conversations with legal, finance, and sales. De-risking, in this reading, "means deliberately attacking all four before you commit engineering capacity, usually through a mix of prototyping, customer interviews, technical spikes, and stakeholder review." Each risk has its own test; the mistake is using one test for all four.

Continuous discovery

The dominant operating model for discovery today is Teresa Torres's continuous discovery, whose central claim is that discovery is a habit, not a phase. The point is to "continuously infuse their product decisions with customer input" in a "structured, and sustainable way" — a steady weekly cadence of customer contact owned by the team building the product, rather than a research project that happens once at the start and is never repeated. Torres's well-known benchmark is a touchpoint with at least one customer every week, conducted by the trio itself.

Her sharpest correction is about why you talk to customers at all: "The purpose of interviewing is NOT to ask what to build. It's to discover and explore opportunities." This inverts the naive model where research is a requirements-gathering exercise. Customers are authorities on their problems, not on your solutions; the interview's job is to surface unmet needs and the contexts they arise in, from which the team — not the customer — derives solutions. A team that asks customers to design the product has outsourced the one job discovery exists to do.

The economic argument is what makes continuous discovery more than a research preference. As Torres frames it, "Agile helped teams build more software - but agile doesn't help us build the right thing. The fruit of discovery work is the time saved not building something that won't work." Continuous discovery is justified not by the features it produces but by the features it prevents — the quarters of engineering effort not spent on solutions that research would have killed in a week. That is the return that pays for the weekly habit.

Dual-track agile and its evolution

Before "continuous discovery" became the dominant term, the same idea traveled under the name dual-track agile. Per ProdPad, it "integrates the activities of product discovery and delivery into two synchronized tracks" run concurrently: a discovery track focused on "pinpointing user issues, pain points and problems, and performing experiments to validate your potential solutions," and a delivery track handling "the actual creation and refinement of the product based on validated insights from the Discovery track." The framing's value was making explicit that discovery is continuous and parallel to delivery, not a gate that happens before it.

The lineage matters for understanding the idea's pedigree. Dual-track "originated from the collaborative efforts of" Jeff Patton and Marty Cagan, with Patton introducing it around 2007, building on earlier work by Desiree Sy at Autodesk. It was a deliberate corrective to the waterfall instinct that survived inside agile teams — the assumption that someone hands engineering a validated spec and the team's only job is to build it well. Dual-track insisted that deciding what to build is itself ongoing team work.

The term has since largely given way to continuous discovery, and the shift is more than fashion. As LogRocket observes, "Continuous discovery has essentially replaced dual-track agile over the last couple of years," because the two-track metaphor implied a cleaner separation than reality supports — "Discovery and delivery are intertwined" and "discovery feeds delivery and delivery feeds discovery." The newer framing keeps the parallelism but drops the implication that the tracks are separable lanes; in practice the same people move fluidly between learning and building, often within the same day. The goal is unchanged: discovery "aims to help product teams build the right things right and quickly test assumptions to reduce waste and mitigate the risk of building the wrong thing."

Opportunity solution trees

If continuous discovery is the habit, the opportunity solution tree is the artifact that keeps it honest. Torres describes it as "a simple way of visually representing the paths you might take to reach a desired outcome," and its structure is the whole point. The hierarchy runs top to bottom: "The root of the tree is your desired outcome... Next is the opportunity space... Below the opportunity space is the solution space... Below the solution space are assumption tests." Each layer constrains the one below it.

Product School's breakdown spells out what each level demands. The Outcome is "the ultimate goal... clear, measurable, and focused on delivering value." Opportunities "represent unmet customer needs or pain points that, if addressed, could move the team closer to the desired outcome." Solutions "are the ideas or features that the team believes will address the identified opportunities." And Experiments "are tests that help validate whether a proposed solution is effective." The tree is a forcing function against the discipline's most persistent failure: jumping straight to solutions.

The structural discipline is that "every experiment is retrofitted to a specific goal." A solution that cannot be attached to an opportunity, or an opportunity that cannot be attached to the outcome, is by construction out of scope — the tree makes orphaned pet features visible. It also reframes prioritization as choosing between opportunities rather than between solutions, which is a higher-leverage decision: picking the right problem to solve matters more than picking the best solution to the wrong one. The tree is where strategy (the outcome) and research (the opportunities) and delivery (the solutions) are forced into a single coherent picture.

Generative versus evaluative research

The research that feeds discovery divides cleanly into two modes, and confusing them is a common source of wasted effort. Mapped onto Nielsen Norman's design cycle, generative research "refers to the methods your team implements in the Discover and Explore stages... It seeks to uncover a deeper understanding of your users to identify opportunities for more valuable solutions" — field studies, interviews, diary studies, journey mapping. Evaluative research, by contrast, "analyzes potential usability issues to validate that your product is aligned with real users... in the Test and Listen stages" — usability testing, benchmark testing, surveys against a concrete artifact.

The distinction is about what question you are asking, not which method you use. Codecademy frames it by intent: generative research is for "developing a deeper understanding of your users and defining the problem" where "the world is your oyster," exploring "if there even is a problem to solve." Evaluative research is to "assess whether a product or concept meets expectations and requirements" and crucially "requires something tangible to evaluate and test, like an existing design or prototype." Generative is "more open-ended" while evaluative "has a defined objective."

The practical error this prevents is running evaluative research when you needed generative, and vice versa. Usability-testing a prototype tells you whether people can use your solution; it tells you nothing about whether the underlying problem was worth solving — that is a generative question answered before a prototype exists. Conversely, open-ended discovery interviews are the wrong tool once you have a specific design to pressure-test. In opportunity-solution-tree terms, generative research populates the opportunity space and evaluative research lives down at the assumption-test leaves; using one where the other belongs is how teams produce confident answers to the wrong questions.

Customer development — the lineage

Modern discovery inherits its DNA from Steve Blank's customer development, the methodology that launched the lean startup movement. Its foundational discipline, per the Kauffman framing, is that "customer development is done almost entirely outside the building in front of customers and partners" — the famous insistence that there are no facts inside the building, only opinions, so validation has to happen in front of real people. Customer development "really is a formal process of how you test all of your hypotheses."

Blank's sharpest contribution to the discovery mindset is the distinction between learning and execution. "A founder's job whose job is learning and discovery" is categorically different from "a salesperson whose job is execution" — the early-stage task is not to sell a known thing efficiently but to discover what the thing should be at all. This is the conceptual ancestor of the discovery-versus-delivery split: Blank drew the line at the company level (search for a business model versus execute one), and the product canon inherited it at the feature level (discover what to build versus deliver it).

The throughline from Blank to Torres is unbroken. "Get out of the building" became "talk to a customer every week"; "test all of your hypotheses" became assumption mapping and the riskiest-assumption test; "search for a business model" became "discover the opportunities that move the outcome." What changed is scale and cadence — Blank's customer development was a founder's full-time search, while continuous discovery is a standing team habit — but the epistemology is identical: your beliefs about customers are hypotheses until evidence from real customers says otherwise.

Assumption mapping — finding the riskiest belief

The bridge between having ideas and testing them is making your assumptions explicit, and the canonical tool is David Bland's assumption mapping. It is "a team exercise where desirability, viability, and feasibility hypotheses are made explicit and prioritized in terms of importance and evidence." The first move — surfacing the beliefs a solution silently depends on — is itself valuable, because the most dangerous assumptions are the ones a team holds so confidently it never notices they are assumptions at all.

The prioritization mechanism is a 2×2: "The x-axis measures Evidence (have evidence/no evidence), while the y-axis measures Importance (important/unimportant)." The action follows directly from the position. "The hypotheses in the top right quadrant contain your beliefs that are critical for success and yet have the least amount of evidence to support them. You'll want your teams to focus their near term experimentation here." Important-and-evidenced beliefs can be monitored; unimportant ones can be ignored; the only quadrant that earns an experiment right now is important-and-unknown.

This is the antidote to the most common testing failure, which is running experiments on whatever is easiest to test rather than whatever is riskiest to be wrong about. Bland's warning is explicit: "You'll need to focus your teams' experimentation efforts on high risk areas. Otherwise, you'll miss your window of opportunity and end up wasting your time." The map turns a vague pile of optimism into a ranked queue of experiments, and the top of that queue is always the belief that, if false, kills the idea.

Hypotheses and experiments

Once the riskiest assumption is identified, discovery becomes the practice of testing it as cheaply as possible. Gothelf and Seiden's Lean UX reframes all product work as testable claims — the canvas has teams "declaring assumptions as we work through" it — and gives the claim a structure: "We believe that this business outcome will be achieved if this user attains this benefit with this feature." Casting a feature idea in this form converts a decision-to-build into a hypothesis-to-test, with a metric, a user, a benefit, and a solution all made falsifiable in one sentence.

The governing principle is economy of learning. Lean UX's central question is "What's the least amount of work we need to do to learn the next most important thing?" — and the build-measure-learn tradition sharpens it. Eric Ries's MVP "is not necessarily the smallest product imaginable... it is simply the fastest way to get through the Build-Measure-Learn feedback loop with the minimum amount of effort," and the discovery-relevant insight is that the loop often needs no product at all: "There are endless ways to test your assumptions before actually building anything," because "the goal is to achieve maximum learning for the least amount of effort."

The sharpest expression of this is the riskiest-assumption test, which deliberately diverges from the MVP. Where "the MVP... tends to focus on previewing strengths and building on success, the RAT is all about testing weaknesses upfront," running "learn-test" cycles before any build. In practice a RAT "will often take the form of targeted experiments such as a landing page, survey, or narrow prototype, rather than anything resembling the product itself." The discipline across all three — Lean UX, build-measure-learn, RAT — is the same: do not build to learn what an experiment could have told you for a fraction of the cost.

Escaping the build trap

All of this discovery machinery exists to escape one organizational failure, which Melissa Perri named the build trap. It is the state of teams "defining and shipping software with no measures of success... focusing on getting features out the door," "too focused on building any thing rather than the right thing." The trap is seductive because output is visible and measurable — features shipped, velocity, story points — while outcomes are slower and harder to attribute, so organizations optimize the thing they can see.

Perri's key insight is that the build trap is structural, not a failure of individual diligence. The escape is organizational: "we need to create a customer-centric organisation that rewards learning and reaching outcomes, rather than focusing on outputs." A team embedded in a system that counts features will produce features no matter how much it believes in discovery, because the incentives overwhelm the intentions. Discovery is the mechanism by which an outcome mandate becomes concrete — it is how "increase retention" turns into a ranked set of validated bets rather than a wish.

This is where discovery connects upward to strategy and goal-setting. The build trap is what happens when a roadmap of features is mistaken for a plan to achieve outcomes; discovery is the discipline that keeps the chain — outcome to opportunity to solution to evidence — intact. Without it, even an organization that has adopted outcomes-based goals will quietly revert to output, because the opportunity-solution tree is the only artifact that makes the difference visible at the level where features are actually chosen.

Common pitfalls

The dominant failure mode in discovery is not skipping it but doing it badly in a way that feels rigorous. The chief culprit, per Mind the Product, is confirmation bias — "Looking for evidence that proves you right, and avoiding or ignoring evidence that contradicts your beliefs." A team that has fallen in love with a solution will run research that confirms it: leading interview questions, demos to friendly customers, metrics chosen after the fact. The output looks like validation and is actually theater.

The structural defense is specifying success criteria before the test, and it is conspicuously underused — "not enough people talk about success criteria, or detail how it is they'll know when they've solved the problem." Deciding in advance what result would count as failure ("at least 3 of 10 target users complete the task unaided") is what converts an experiment from a confirmation ritual into a genuine test, because it removes the post-hoc freedom to interpret any outcome as success. An experiment whose pass bar is set after seeing the data has tested nothing.

The deeper warning is about what discovery is for. "You might have a groundbreaking idea, but without proper discovery, you won't know if it's the right one for your users" — the value of discovery is in the disconfirmation it makes possible, not the confidence it provides. A discovery process that never kills an idea is not a discovery process; it is an elaborate way of feeling certain. The teams that get the most from discovery are the ones most willing to be told they are wrong, early and cheaply.

Choosing how much discovery

The frameworks in this primer are not alternatives to choose between; they compose into a single pipeline, and the practitioner judgment is mostly about how much rather than which. The amount of discovery a decision warrants scales with its risk and its reversibility. A cheap, easily reversed change needs little — ship it and watch the metric. A large, hard-to-reverse bet that depends on an unproven value assumption warrants the full apparatus: opportunity solution tree, assumption mapping, and a riskiest-assumption test before a line of production code.

The sequencing is consistent across the canon. Start from an outcome, not a feature. Use generative research to populate the opportunity space — to find which problems are real and worth solving — before generating solutions. Map the assumptions each candidate solution depends on, and rank them by Bland's importance-versus-evidence test. Run the cheapest experiment that can falsify the riskiest assumption, using evaluative methods once there is something tangible to test. Promote a solution to delivery only when its load-bearing assumptions have survived contact with real users. Each stage exists to avoid wasting the next stage's far greater cost.

The meta-skill is calibration. Too little discovery and you are in the build trap, shipping confident guesses; too much and you have replaced the build trap with analysis paralysis, where research becomes its own form of not-deciding. The canon's answer is to let risk set the dial — spend discovery effort in proportion to what it would cost to be wrong — and to remember that the goal is never certainty, which is unavailable, but enough evidence to make the next commitment responsibly.

Live debates — where practitioners disagree

Several questions in discovery are genuinely contested, and a primer that presented them as settled would mislead.

How much discovery is enough? This is the most persistent live debate. The continuous-discovery camp argues for a standing weekly cadence regardless of project; pragmatists counter that discovery effort should scale with risk and that a mandated weekly interview can become its own ritual divorced from any live decision. Both agree discovery is essential and disagree on whether a fixed cadence or a risk-indexed one is the right default — this is unresolved.

Do cheap AI prototypes help or hurt? A newer and sharpening disagreement: the collapse in the cost of building prototypes (AI-assisted) makes it trivial to put something tangible in front of a customer. One camp sees this as a discovery accelerant; skeptics, including voices in the Torres tradition, warn it intensifies confirmation bias — a leader demos an AI prototype to one enthusiastic customer and declares it validated, which is theater, not evidence. Practitioners actively disagree on whether cheaper prototyping raises or lowers discovery quality.

Who owns discovery? The product-trio model holds that discovery is shared work owned jointly by product, design, and engineering. Others argue this dilutes accountability or is impractical in teams without a full trio, and that a dedicated researcher or the PM alone should drive it. The canon leans trio, but the question of whether that model generalizes beyond well-resourced product orgs remains contested.

RAT versus MVP. Whether the riskiest-assumption test should displace the MVP as the default first move is itself argued. RAT advocates say the MVP encourages building too early; MVP defenders say a real (if minimal) product surfaces behavioral evidence that surveys and landing pages cannot. The two optimize for different evidence, and which to reach for first is a live methodological dispute, not a settled best practice.

Where this connects — adjacent topics

Discovery sits between strategy and delivery; the threads below are where it connects to the rest of the discipline.

  • User research & interviewing technique — the craft layer beneath generative research: how to run a non-leading interview, recruit well, and synthesize findings (the technique, where this primer covers the process).
  • Product strategy & vision — the upstream source of the outcome that roots every opportunity solution tree; discovery executes against the diagnosis strategy sets.
  • Roadmapping & prioritization — the downstream layer that sequences the validated bets discovery produces; opportunity scoring and the OST's opportunity ranking are the handoff.
  • Outcomes vs. outputs & metrics — the measurement discipline that defines what discovery is trying to move and supplies the success criteria experiments are judged against.
  • Jobs-to-be-done — a complementary lens for the opportunity space, framing unmet needs as jobs customers are trying to get done.
  • Experimentation & A/B testing — the quantitative end of evaluative research, where assumption tests on live traffic become controlled experiments.

References

  1. producttalk.org ·cited· (T1)

    Teresa Torres's opportunity solution tree is "a simple way of visually representing the paths you might take to reach a desired outcome." Its hierarchy is load-bearing: "The root of the tree is your desired outcome... Next is the opportunity space... Below the opportunity space is the solution space... Below the solution space are assumption tests." It exists to keep product trios honest — "Build and maintain a shared understanding of how they might reach their desired outcome" and "Unlock faster learning cycles" — by forcing solutions to attach to a validated opportunity.

  2. strategyzer.com ·cited· (T1)

    David Bland's assumptions mapping is "a team exercise where desirability, viability, and feasibility hypotheses are made explicit and prioritized in terms of importance and evidence." It plots assumptions on a 2x2 — "The x-axis measures Evidence (have evidence/no evidence), while the y-axis measures Importance" — and the action is to test the top-right quadrant first: "beliefs that are critical for success and yet have the least amount of evidence to support them. You'll want your teams to focus their near term experimentation here."

  3. jeffgothelf.com ·cited· (T1)

    Jeff Gothelf and Josh Seiden's Lean UX reframes product work as testable assumptions — the canvas has you "declaring assumptions as we work through" it. The hypothesis statement gives structure: "We believe that this business outcome will be achieved if this user attains this benefit with this feature," connecting metric, segment, benefit, and solution into one falsifiable claim. The governing question is economy of learning: "What's the least amount of work we need to do to learn the next most important thing?"

  4. productboard.com ·cited· (T2)

    Productboard frames discovery via Cagan's two questions — "you need to discover whether there are real users out there that want this product" and then "a product solution to this problem that is usable, useful, and feasible." The purpose is risk reduction: "We conduct product discovery because we want to reduce the risks around what we decide to build," and discovery precedes and complements delivery — teams "research and validate concepts before sending them to engineering" rather than validating after launch.

  5. roadmap.one ·cited· (T2)

    Marty Cagan's four big risks, the canonical map of what discovery must attack: value risk ("Will customers buy it or users choose to use it"), usability risk ("Can users figure out how to use it"), feasibility risk ("Can engineering build it with the available time, skills, and tech"), and business viability risk ("Does it work for legal, finance, sales, marketing, and brand"). The discipline is that "Discovery exists to kill all four before delivery starts" — "deliberately attacking all four before you commit engineering capacity."

  6. andrewclark.co.uk ·cited· (T2)

    Teresa Torres's Continuous Discovery Habits reframes discovery as an ongoing team habit — "continuously infuse their product decisions with customer input" in a "structured, and sustainable way" — rather than a phase. Her sharpest correction of research intent: "The purpose of interviewing is NOT to ask what to build. It's to discover and explore opportunities." And the economic case: "Agile helped teams build more software - but agile doesn't help us build the right thing. The fruit of discovery work is the time saved not building something that won't work."

  7. blog.logrocket.com ·cited· (T2)

    LogRocket marks the generational shift in vocabulary: "Continuous discovery has essentially replaced dual-track agile over the last couple of years," because the two-track metaphor understated how tightly the work interleaves — "Discovery and delivery are intertwined" and "discovery feeds delivery and delivery feeds discovery." The shared goal is de-risking: continuous discovery "aims to help product teams build the right things right and quickly test assumptions to reduce waste and mitigate the risk of building the wrong thing."

  8. prodpad.com ·cited· (T2)

    Dual-track agile "integrates the activities of product discovery and delivery into two synchronized tracks" run concurrently: the discovery track pinpoints "user issues, pain points and problems, and performing experiments to validate your potential solutions," while the delivery track handles "the actual creation and refinement of the product based on validated insights from the Discovery track." Origin: it "originated from the collaborative efforts of" Jeff Patton and Marty Cagan, with Patton introducing it in 2007 (building on Desiree Sy's Autodesk work).

  9. productschool.com ·cited· (T2)

    Product School details the OST's four levels: the Outcome is "the ultimate goal... clear, measurable, and focused on delivering value"; Opportunities "represent unmet customer needs or pain points that, if addressed, could move the team closer"; Solutions "are the ideas or features that the team believes will address the identified opportunities"; and Experiments "are tests that help validate whether a proposed solution is effective." The structural payoff: "every experiment is retrofitted to a specific goal," preventing premature solution-jumping by requiring opportunities be found through research first.

  10. dovetail.com ·cited· (T2)

    The generative/evaluative split mapped onto Nielsen Norman's design cycle: generative research "refers to the methods your team implements in the Discover and Explore stages... It seeks to uncover a deeper understanding of your users to identify opportunities for more valuable solutions" (field studies, interviews, diary studies, journey mapping). Evaluative research "analyzes potential usability issues to validate that your product is aligned with real users... in the Test and Listen stages" (usability testing, benchmark testing, surveys). Generative finds the problem; evaluative checks the solution.

  11. codecademy.com ·cited· (T2)

    Codecademy sharpens the distinction by intent: generative research is for "developing a deeper understanding of your users and defining the problem" where "the world is your oyster," and it explores "if there even is a problem to solve." Evaluative research is to "assess whether a product or concept meets expectations and requirements" and requires "something tangible to evaluate and test, like an existing design or prototype." The cleanest framing: generative is "more open-ended" while evaluative "has a defined objective."

  12. entrepreneurship.org ·cited· (T2)

    Steve Blank's customer development is the discovery substrate the product canon inherits: "customer development is done almost entirely outside the building in front of customers and partners," and it "really is a formal process of how you test all of your hypotheses." The founder's role is explicitly learning, not execution — "a founder's job whose job is learning and discovery" — and the famous discipline is that there are no facts inside the building, so validation must happen in front of real customers.

  13. strategyzer.com ·cited· (T2)

    Strategyzer's reading of Eric Ries's build-measure-learn loop, which sits "at the heart of the Lean Startup process," stresses that the MVP "is not necessarily the smallest product imaginable... it is simply the fastest way to get through the Build-Measure-Learn feedback loop with the minimum amount of effort." The counterintuitive lesson for discovery: "There are endless ways to test your assumptions before actually building anything," because "the goal is not to start building a limited version... The goal is to achieve maximum learning for the least amount of effort."

  14. modelthinkers.com ·cited· (T2)

    The Riskiest Assumption Test (RAT) "involves rapidly identifying and testing the riskiest assumptions and potential fail points for any initiative early in the process." It contrasts with the MVP: "the MVP... tends to focus on previewing strengths and building on success, while the RAT is all about testing weaknesses upfront," using "learn-test" cycles before building. In practice a RAT "will often take the form of targeted experiments such as a landing page, survey, or narrow prototype, rather than anything resembling the product itself" — the cheapest possible test of the highest-risk belief.

  15. mindtheproduct.com ·cited· (T2)

    Melissa Perri's build trap is the organizational failure discovery exists to prevent: teams "defining and shipping software with no measures of success... focusing on getting features out the door," "too focused on building any thing rather than the right thing." The escape is structural, not personal: "we need to create a customer-centric organisation that rewards learning and reaching outcomes, rather than focusing on outputs" — making discovery the engine that converts the outcome mandate into validated bets.

  16. mindtheproduct.com ·cited· (T2)

    The most common discovery pitfall is confirmation bias — "Looking for evidence that proves you right, and avoiding or ignoring evidence that contradicts your beliefs" — compounded by the fact that "not enough people talk about success criteria, or detail how it is they'll know when they've solved the problem." The framing warning: "you might have a groundbreaking idea, but without proper discovery, you won't know if it's the right one for your users" — discovery's value is in the disconfirmation it makes possible, not the confirmation teams instinctively seek.

pmos Source: docs/pmos/primer/2026-06-14_product-discovery-and-user-research.html · Created using pmos‑learnkit
